coredata.dc:title: Synergistic photocatalytic activation of permanganate by carbon quantum dots-doped g-C₃N₄ under visible light irradiation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Runshi
Hu, Rongrong
Gao, Liansong
Wei, Huangzhao
Zhao, Shuaiqi
Liu, Xiuying
Li, Xiaoxia
Xu, Aihua
coredata.dc:description: Synergistic photoactivation of permanganate has been reported to be an effective method for degrading pollutants. In this study, we found that the incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on the surface of graphitic carbon nitride (g-C₃N₄) significantly enhanced the visible light response of potassium permanganate (PM), using sulfadiazine (SDZ) as a representative compound. The synergistic photocatalytic system of PM and CQD-doped g-C₃N₄ demonstrated remarkable efficiencies in SDZ degradation, achieving degradation rates that were 35.3 and 9.9 times higher than those observed in Vis/PM and Vis/PM/g-C₃N4 systems, respectively. Mechanistic investigations revealed several key pathways in the degradation process: Photogenerated electrons (e⁻) facilitated the reduction of O₂, resulting in the formation of superoxide anions (O2 -•) and singlet oxygen (1O2) as active species; the photogenerated holes (h⁺) acted as Lewis acid to activate PM to degrade the pollutant directly. While the contribution of intermediate manganese species was not significant in the system. The synergistic photocatalytic system exhibited higher degradation efficiency for electron-rich substrates and exhibited good adaptability in different water conditions. These findings provide new insights into the effectiveness and mechanism of photocatalytic PM activation for environmental remediation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Permanganate
G-C3N4
Carbon quantum dot
Photocatalysis
Dissolved oxygen
Photo-generated hole

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Role of photo-induced electron and hole

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HR TEM images, (b) UV–vis DRS spectra, (c) (ahv)2 versus hv curves, (d) VB XPS spectra and (e) band structures, (f) steady-state photo-luminescence emission spectra and (g) photocurrent responses of g-C3N4, CNC-1 and CNC-2.

(a) Degradation of SDZ by different systems, (b) the corresponding pseudo first-order rate constants (k obs). (c) Degradation of SDZ and (d) the corresponding k obs by photo-induced PM with the different photocatalysts. Condition: [Catalyst]= 0.4 g/L, [PM] = 100 μM, [SDZ] = 40 μM, pHini = 7.0, T = 25 ℃, visible light (λ >400 nm, 100 mW cm−2).

(a) The k obs of SDZ degradation by Vis/PM/CNC-2 system in the presence of various scavengers, (b) BA oxidation in Vis/PM/CNC-2 and Vis/CNC-2 system, (c) The k obs of SDZ degradation by Vis/CNC-2 system in the presence of various scavengers Vis/CNC-2 system. (d) Comparison of SDZ degradation with Vis/PM/CNC-2 an Vis/CNC-2 under aerobic and anaerobic conditions.Condition: [Catalyst] = 0.4 g/L. [SDZ] = 40 μM, [PM] = 100 μM, pHini = 7.0, T = 25 ℃, visible light (λ >400 nm, 100 mW cm−2).

(a) UV-Vis spectra changes for Vis/PM/CNC-2 system. (b) Degradation of SDZ by Vis/PM/CNC-2 system in the presence of PP. (c) Oxidation of PMSO by Vis/PM/CNC-2 system and Vis/PM system. (d) Photocurrent responses of CNC-2 in the presence and absence of PM. (e) Degradation of SDZ by Vis/PM/CNC-2 system in the presence of K2Cr2O7. (f) OCP curves for Vis/PM/CNC-2 and PM/CNC-2 system.

(a) Degradation of various pollutants by Vis/PM/CNC-2 system. Effect of (b) pH, (c) anions and HA, and (d) cations on SDZ degradation by Vis/PM/CNC-2 system. (e) SDZ degradation in various water matrices by Vis/PM/CNC-2 system. (d) Reusability of CNC-2 in Vis/PM/ CNC-2 system. Condition: [Catalyst] = 0.4 g/L. [SDZ] = 40 μM, [PM] = 100 μM, T = 25 ℃.
The possible reaction mechanism in Vis/PM/ CNC-2 system.

head.ce:title: Synergistic photocatalytic activation of permanganate by carbon quantum dots-doped g-C₃N₄ under visible light irradiation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Wuhan Textile University

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Biomass Fibers and Eco-Dyeing & Finishing, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan 430200, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Biomass Fibers and Eco-Dyeing & Finishing, Wuhan Textile University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Key Laboratory of Biomass Fibers and Eco-Dyeing & Finishing, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan, 430200, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan 430200, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Wuhan Textile University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synergistic photoactivation of permanganate has been reported to be an effective method for degrading pollutants. In this study, we found that the incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on the surface of graphitic carbon nitride (g-C₃N₄) significantly enhanced the visible light response of potassium permanganate (PM), using sulfadiazine (SDZ) as a representative compound. The synergistic photocatalytic system of PM and CQD-doped g-C₃N₄ demonstrated remarkable efficiencies in SDZ degradation, achieving degradation rates that were 35.3 and 9.9 times higher than those observed in Vis/PM and Vis/PM/g-C₃N4 systems, respectively. Mechanistic investigations revealed several key pathways in the degradation process: Photogenerated electrons (e⁻) facilitated the reduction of O₂, resulting in the formation of superoxide anions (O2 -•) and singlet oxygen (1O2) as active species; the photogenerated holes (h⁺) acted as Lewis acid to activate PM to degrade the pollutant directly. While the contribution of intermediate manganese species was not significant in the system. The synergistic photocatalytic system exhibited higher degradation efficiency for electron-rich substrates and exhibited good adaptability in different water conditions. These findings provide new insights into the effectiveness and mechanism of photocatalytic PM activation for environmental remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since its discovery in the 19th century, potassium permanganate (KMnO₄, PM) has been widely recognized as a classic strong oxidizing agent. However, conventional oxidation reactions utilizing PM for the degradation of contaminants in water typically require high concentrations, extended reaction times, and appropriate acidic conditions. Therefore, there is a need to develop new methods to enhance the oxidation efficiency of PM for more efficient and economic applications [1].

An increasing number of studies have explored various methods to activate PM and proposed different mechanisms to elucidate the enhanced oxidation efficiency [2]. For example, the colloidal manganese dioxide (cMnO₂) generated during the reduction of PM can act as an auto-catalyst to enhance the activation of PM by producing intermediate manganese (Mnint) species, such as Mn (VI), Mn(V), Mn(IV), and Mn(III), which exhibit higher reactivity than Mn(VII) under specific conditions [3–5]. Additionally, metal ions such as Fe(II) and Ru(III), or metal oxide like Co₃O₄, can enhance the oxidation of PM by generating highly active Mnint species to degrade pollutants [6–8]. Furthermore, certain ligands, including pyrophosphate (PP), humic acid (HA), and epigallocatechin gallate, have been shown to form complexes with the unstable Mnint species, thereby enhancing the overall oxidation efficiency of PM [9–11]. In a recent study, Zhou et al. found that in neutral and alkaline solutions, HA-activated PM to degrade diclofenac through electron transfer [12]. The introduction of reducing reagents such as sulfite, hydroxylamine and quinone [13–15], or redox mediators like 1-hydroxy benzotriazole and TEMPO [16,17], as well as carbon materials like graphite and biochar [18–20], can facilitate electron transfer from pollutants to PM and then boost the oxidation process. Recently, our group revealed that α-MnO2, NiO and Co3O4 acted as Lewis acids to enhance the oxidation potential of PM and initiate the electron transfer between PM and pollutants [21,22]. Except for the direct catalytic activation of PM, the combination of PM activation with other advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) is another way to expand its application potential [23–25].

Photocatalysis addresses energy scarcity and environmental pollution using semiconductors [26], and the integration of photocatalysis and oxidizing agents is commonly recognized to be chemical and energy-intensive [27]. It has been reported that visible light was able to initiate the reduction of PM, with the generated Mnint, hydroxyl radicals (•OH), or superoxide radicals (O2 -•) as active species [28]. Li et al. recently reported that indium oxide (In2O3) accelerated the activation of PM under visible light irradiation for flumequine degradation and the generated Mnint and •OH were verified as the main active species [29]. Graphitic phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has attracted much attention as a metal-free photocatalyst. Zhang et al. reported that g-C3N4 could enhance the visible light response of PM and found that photogenerated electrons (e-) of g-C3N4 initiated the conversion of PM to reactive Mnint species to remove pollutants [30]. After modifying with nitrogen vacancy, the performance of PM activation with g-C3N4 under visible light irradiation for sulfadiazine degradation was enhanced significantly [31]. Nonetheless, insufficient visible-light absorption, sluggish charge transport, and the rapid recombination of e- and photogenerated holes (h+), severely restrict the catalytic efficiency of g-C3N4 [29,32]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a proper modification strategy for g-C₃N₄ to improve its synergistic photocatalytic activation with PM.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as a powerful tool for enhancing photocatalytic performance. As electron acceptors, CQDs not only reduce the recombination rate of e- - h+ pairs in g-C₃N₄ by facilitating the separation and transfer of e- but also enhance light absorption capabilities of g-C₃N₄ by narrowing the band gap [33,34]. Liu et al. reported that the degradation of diclofenac by the CQD-anchored g-C3N4 was 15 times higher than that of pure g-C3N4 [35]. Given the success of CQDs in enhancing the photocatalytic properties of g-C₃N₄, it is expected that the CQD-doped g-C₃N₄ will accelerate the activation of PM under visible light irradiation. However, the effectiveness of this system for pollutant degradation is still uncertain, and the underlying mechanisms require further elucidation. In this study, to develop an efficient photocatalyst for PM activation and to gain a deeper understanding of the synergistic photocatalytic mechanism, CQD-doped g-C3N4 was first synthesized, and the physicochemical properties were evaluated. Then sulfadiazine was used as a representative pollutant for the performance of the Vis/PM/CQD-doped g-C₃N₄ system. The mechanisms involved in the enhanced photocatalytic degradation were thoroughly discussed. Finally, the potential impacts of several factors on the application in real water bodies were examined.
Text S1 in Supplementary Information lists the detailed information of the involved chemicals.

CQDs were synthesized with a modified method reported in the literature [35]. 5.0 g of citric acid monohydrate and 1.66 g of urea were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized (DI) water. After stirring for 15 min, the solution was transferred to a high-pressure reactor equipped with a PTFE liner and heated in an oven to 180 ℃ for 5 h. Subsequently, the cooled mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min to remove the bulk particles at the bottom. The resulting CQDs solution was stored at 4 °C for use in the subsequent experimental steps.

CQD-doped g-C3N4 was synthesized by a thermal polymerization method [36]. 10 g of urea was first dispersed in 25 mL of DI water, and mixed with a certain volume of CQDs solution. After thorough ultrasonic mixing for 1 h, the solution was dried at 70 °C overnight to get a brown solid. This solid was then heated to 550 °C with a rate of 5 °C/min and maintained for 3 h. The obtained samples were designated as CNC-1 and CNC-2, corresponding to the addition of 20 µL and 50 µL of CQDs stock solution, respectively.
The details of characterization methods are provided in Text S2.

The photocatalytic degradation experiments were conducted at 25 ± 1 °C using a photoreactor equipped with a 300 W Xenon lamp (λ > 400 nm). The light intensity was detected to be 100 mW cm−2. A 50 mL of SDZ solution (40 µM) containing 100 µM of PM was prepared and magnetically stirred under light irradiation for 10 min. 20 mg of catalyst was added into the solution to initiate the photocatalytic reaction. At given time intervals, 1 mL of the suspension was collected, and filtered through a 0.45 µm membrane. Hydroxylamine hydrochloride (10 mM) was added to stop the reaction. Unless otherwise specified, all experiments were performed at the initial pH (7.0) without adjustment. All degradation efficiency experiments were performed in triplicate (n = 3) to ensure data reliability. The analytical methods for pollutant degradation, radical detection, and other analyses are provided in Table S1 and Text S3.

The XRD pattern of CQDs in Fig. S1 with a wild and weak peak at 2theta of about 26° confirms the successful synthesis of the quantum dots. The incorporation of CQDs, has no discernible impact on the lamellar surface structure of g-C₃N₄, from the SEM images shown in Fig. S2. But upon close examination of the HR TEM images, especially from the enlarged figures in Fig. 1a, it can be observed that CQDs with approximately 10 nm in size were visible on the multilamellar pleated structure of g-C₃N₄ (labeled with a yellow arrow), which are significantly more abundant in CNC-2 than those in CNC-1. The XRD pattern, FTIR spectrum and N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms analysis of CNC-1 and CNC-2 were almost identical to that of pristine g-C₃N₄, indicating that the doping of CQDs did not influence the crystal structure, surface area and chemical bonding of g-C3N4 (Fig. S3a-c, Table S2). From the XPS analysis in Fig. S3d and Table S2, the molar ratio of C/N increased from 0.857 to 0.858 and 0.868 in g-C3N4, CNC-1 and CNC-2, respectively, further indicating the presence of CQDs in the catalysts [35]. The characteristic sp2 hybrid carbon (N-CN) in C1s of g-C3N4 at 288.3 eV did not shift after the doping of CQDs (Fig. S3e). However, the peak of C-C/CC bond slightly shifted from 286.3 to 286.4 eV in CNC-1 and CNC-2 compared with g-C3N4. This may be attributed to the π-π stacking interactions between the CQDs and g-C₃N₄ [35]. The comprehensive analysis shows that CQDs were deposited on the surface of g-C3N4, with the original structure of g-C3N4 remained essentially unchanged.

The photoelectric property of the catalysts was investigated by spectroscopic techniques and electrochemical tests. The UV–vis DRS spectra depicted in Fig. 1b illustrate that the modification of CQDs significantly enhanced the visible light absorption of g-C3N4. Utilizing the Tauc plot method, the band gaps (Eg) of g-C₃N₄, CNC-1, and CNC-2 were determined to be 2.79 eV, 2.61 eV, and 2.41 eV, respectively (Fig. 1c). It means that the electrons in the valence band (VB) of the CQD-doped g-C3N4 can be excited by absorbing lower-energy photons, and then improve its light absorption capabilities. Further analysis of the catalysts' energy band structure was conducted through valence band XPS (VB-XPS) measurements. The VB maxima (EVB) for g-C3N4, CNC-1, and CNC-2 were 2.47, 2.39, and 2.31 eV, respectively (Fig. 1d). When utilizing the equation ENHE = Φ + EVB - 4.44 to convert the EVB into the potential relative to the standard hydrogen electrode (ENHE), where Φ is the 3.88 eV of work function [37], the calculated VB potential values for g-C3N4, CNC-1, and CNC-2 were 1.91, 1.83, and 1.75 eV, respectively. The energy band structure of g-C3N4, CNC-1, and CNC-2 is schematically depicted in Fig. 1e. It is clear that as the content of CQDs increased, not only the Eg of g-C₃N₄ narrowed significantly, but the position of the VB also shifted to higher energy levels. These changes will enhance the light absorption capacity and charge separation efficiency of g-C₃N₄.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was employed to investigate the recombination of photoinduced carriers. A lower intensity of the PL peak indicates a reduced rate of recombination of photoinduced e--h+ pairs, suggesting more efficient charge separation and transfer. As shown in Fig. 1f, the PL intensity of CNC-1 and CNC-2 were significantly lower than that of g-C3N4, indicating that the introduction of CQDs resulted in slow photogenerated e-- h+ pair recombination, and enabled an efficient electron transport and charge separation processes. The photocurrent response of the catalysts was further investigated by measuring the instantaneous photocurrent. From Fig. 1g, it was found that CNC-1 and CNC-2 exhibited higher photocurrent intensity than pure g-C3N4, and the current intensity of CNC-2 was 1.97 times higher than that of g-C3N4, indicating that CQDs doping promoted the generated charge carrier transfer and separation. In summary, the doping of CQDs significantly improved the photoelectric properties of g-C3N4, which may endow the compounds with excellent photocatalytic performance.

The performance of synergistic photocatalytic PM activation with CQD-doped g-C3N4 was investigated by the degradation of SDZ. As shown in Fig. 2a, the removal of SDZ by PM oxidation induced by visible light was only 5 % within 30 min, whereas the degradation efficiency reached 59 % when using CNC-2 as a photocatalyst. When combining PM and CNC-2 induced photocatalytic system (Vis/PM/CNC-2), the degradation efficiency significantly reached 98 %. The pseudo-first-order rate constant (k
obs ) of SDZ by Vis/PM/CNC-2 system was 0.106 min−1, which was 35.3 and 5.6 times that in Vis/PM (0.003 min−1) and Vis/CNC-2 (0.019 min−1) (Fig. S4, Fig. 2b). When compared with g-C₃N₄, it is evident that CQD-doped g-C₃N₄ activated PM exhibited much higher photocatalytic efficiency (Fig. 2c and d), and the k
obs by Vis/PM/CNC-2 system was 9.9 and 4.0 times of that in Vis/g-C₃N₄ and Vis/PM/g-C₃N₄ systems, respectively. As shown in Fig. S5, the degradation efficiency was significantly enhanced by the addition of PM, regardless of whether the g-C₃N₄ was primitive or doped. Furthermore, the enhancement was not from the CQD photocatalyzed PM activation, because the k
obs in Vis/PM/CQDs system was only 0.0048 min−1 (Fig. S5). However, the total organic carbon of SDZ solution was slightly increased from 6.5 to 11.2 mg/L after the reaction with Vis/PM/CNC-2 system, probably caused by the dissolution of g-C₃N₄ into the solution.

The effects of PM concentration and catalyst dosage on SDZ degradation were presented in Fig. S6a, the addition of 25 µM of PM significantly enhanced the photocatalytic activity of CNC-2, with SDZ removal increased from 45 % to 77 % within 30 min. However, when the concentration of PM exceeded 100 µM, the degradation was plateaued, possibly because excessive PM hindered the light absorption of CNC-2. As the CNC-2 dosage increased from 0 to 1.0 g/L, the enhanced from 5 % to 100 % within 20 min (Fig. S6b), due to the formation of more e- and h+ and then more active species.

There may be several pathways for the photocatalytic system integrated by the synergistic effect of CQD-doped g-C₃N₄ and PM: reactive oxygen species (ROS) from visible light irradiated CQD-doped g-C₃N₄, reactive Mnint species from PM, and photo-generated e- and h+ [35,38]. The following section will systematically detect the possible reactive species and elucidate their contributions.

The active species were first explored using various quenchers. The addition of MeOH or TBA into Vis/PM/CNC-2 system had a slight inhibiting effect on the degradation of SDZ (Fig. S7 and Fig. 3a), indicating that the contribution of •OH was minimal. Moreover, benzoic acid, a probe for •OH, was not degraded in the system, which also suggests the absence of •OH (Fig. 3b), despite the detection of 1:2:2:1 signal of DMPO-OH by EPR (Fig. S8a). For Vis/CNC-2 system, nearly no decline of the degradation rate was observed in the presence of MeOH and TBA (Fig. 3c). NBT and NaN3 were used as probes to detect O2 -• and 1O2, respectively. As can be seen in Fig. S7 and Fig. 3a, SDZ degradation was strongly inhibited by the scavengers. The presence of both reactive species was also confirmed by EPR in Fig. S8b and c. Comparing the normalized k obs (ratio of k obs with scavenger to k obs without scavenger) in Vis/PM/CNC-2 system to that in Vis/PM system, NBT exhibited a much stronger inhibition in Vis/PM/CNC-2 system, illustrating more O2 -• species were generated in the system, and it may be the precursor of 1O2, As analyzed above, the values of CB and VB of CNC-2 were −0.66 eV and 1.75 eV, respectively. Based on the redox potential of O2/ O2 -• (-0.33 V), it is judged that the photo-induced e- can reduce O2 into O2 -•. To verify this assumption, N2 was continuously pumped into Vis/PM/CNC-2 and Vis/CNC-2 systems to deplete dissolved oxygen (DO) during the reaction. As illustrated in Fig. 3d, the Vis/CNC-2 system exhibited minimal activity under anaerobic conditions (N₂ atmosphere), highlighting that O2 -• was predominantly dependent on the presence of O₂ in the Vis/CNC-2 system. In contrast, the removal of SDZ in Vis/PM/CNC-2 system dropped from 96 % to 43 %, indicating that while O₂ plays a crucial role in Vis/PM/CNC-2 system. Furthermore, the system can still function effectively in the absence of O₂, suggesting there may be an alternative pathway for SDZ degradation.

UV–vis spectroscopy was employed to detect the change of PM at 525 nm and Mn(IV) in the form of cMnO2 at 370 nm. In the Vis/PM/CNC-2 system, the absorption intensity in the UV and visible regions continuously decreased as time extended ( Fig. 4a); whereas in the Vis/PM and PM/CNC-2 systems, the absorption intensity remained almost unchanged (Fig. S9a and b). This indicates that the adsorption of PM on CNC-2 or transformation of PM only occurred in the presence of both CNC-2 and light radiation. Notably, the addition of SDZ led to a significant reduction in the absorbance of 525 nm (Fig. S9c), suggesting that SDZ enhanced the adsorption and reduction of PM, which will be further discussed later. As observed from Fig. S9d, a slight increase near 370 nm at the beginning of the reaction indicates the presence of a trace of cMnO₂ in the system. Studies have reported that cMnO₂ can activate PM to produce active species and can also directly oxidize pollutants [4,39]. To evaluate the contribution of the cMnO₂ in the system, it was synthesized and added to the Vis/PM/CNC-2 system. The results in Fig. S10 indicated that the enhancement of SDZ degradation was not significant, suggesting that the trace amounts of cMnO₂ produced during the reaction are not the primary active species.

Based on the specific complexation of PP with Mn(III), PP was utilized to detect Mn(III) in the system. The degradation was promoted by 0.5 mM of PP, and a weak inhibition occurred at 5 mM of PP (Fig. 4b). After adding PP in the Vis/PM/CNC-2 system, the absorbance at 270 nm for Mn(III)-PP was not elevated significantly, compared with that in the absence of PP, indicating that there were not many Mn(III) species produced. PMSO was chosen as a probe to clarify the existence of Mn(V). As shown in Fig. 4c, PMSO was readily oxidized to PMSO2 by PM upon visible light irradiation. However, the presence of CNC-2 in the Vis/PM system did not enhance the conversion of PMSO, ruling out the contribution of Mn(V). All the results suggested that the contribution of Mnint was not significant in Vis/PM/CNC-2 system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of photo-induced electron and hole
ce:section.ce:para: As observed from the photocurrent response in Fig. 4d, the addition of PM to the Vis/CNC-2 resulted in a 2.7-fold increase in current intensity, despite the concentration of PM did not decrease significantly (Fig. 4a). The enhancement of photocurrent means that synergistic effect of CNC-2 and PM can more effectively separate and transfer photogenerated e- and h+, and inhibit their recombination. Here PM may act as an electron acceptor and promote the transfer of photogenerated e-. In order to verify this hypothesis, K2Cr2O7 was then employed as an electron scavenger. It is unexpected from Fig. 4e that the degradation of SDZ in both Vis/CNC-2 and Vis/PM/CNC-2 systems was only slightly inhibited by K2Cr2O7, suggesting that photo-generated e- was not the key initiator in our experiment, which is inconsistent with another study [30]. Besides photo-induced e-, the concomitantly generated h+ is another possible active species that can react with PM. EDTA, a well-known hole scavenger, was utilized to detect h+. In both Vis/PM/CNC-2 and Vis/CNC-2 systems, EDTA exhibited inhibitory effects on the degradation of SDZ (Fig. 3a and c). Especially, the k Scavenger/k Control ratio was 0.19 in the Vis/CNC-2 system, whereas it was only 0.08 in the Vis/PM/CNC-2 system, indicating substantial generation of h+ in Vis/PM/CNC-2 system.

The potential of photo-generated h+ is as high as 1.40 V, and has been verified as an important species in the CQD-modified g-C3N4 photocatalytic process [33]. KI is a strong reducing agent and can be reacted with both PM and h+. The faster the disappearance of KI into the system, the more PM or h+ it contains. From Fig. S11, it is clear that the consumption of KI (Abs at 225 nm) was much faster in the Vis/PM/CNC-2 system than in the Vis/PM system, suggesting more generated h+. The increase in open-circuit potential (OCP) from 0.48 V to 0.6 V upon the addition of PM in the Vis/CNC-2 system indicates a higher potential in Vis/PM/CNC-2 system (Fig. 4f). It was reported in our previous studies that the Lewis acids will complex with MnO4 - that with a higher potential to enable pollutant degradation through electron transfer to degrade the electronic-rich pollutants, rather than generating Mnint species [21,40]. This means that the photogenerated h+ did not act as an oxidizing agent directly, but rather function as Lewis acids to enhance the oxidation potential of PM, which would oxidize the pollutant directly. This result also confirms the fact that less Mn(III) and MnO2 were generated in the aforementioned experiments.

Based on the above analysis, the mechanism of PM activation in the Vis/PM/CN-2 system was proposed in Scheme 1. After doping with CQDs, g-C₃N₄ exhibits better photoelectric properties, such as enhanced visible light absorption, a narrower Eg, and improved charge separation efficiency. Thus, under visible light irradiation, the photogenerated e- and h+ were produced and separated efficiently. Through the reduction of e-, O2 is converted to O2 -•, and further transforms to 1O2. Simultaneously, the photogenerated h+ acts as Lewis acid to enhance the oxidation potential of PM, making the latter easier to directly oxidize pollutants. Moreover, rapid consumption of h+ by PM also enhances the separation efficiency of e- and h+ and inhibits their recombination. The produced O2 -•, 1O2 and PM complex then take part in the degradation of SDZ, while the role of formed Mnint in the system was minor, in good agreement with nitrogen vacancy-modified g-C3N4 [31].

The Vis/PM/CNC-2 system was utilized to degrade different organic pollutants, including SDZ, PN, LVF, SMX, PNP, BPA and 4-CP. As shown in Fig. 5a, the electronic-rich BPA and 4-CP were almost completely removed within 10 min. On the contrary, the electron-deficient PNP was slowly degraded. This selective organic oxidation pattern is consistent with the performance of Lewis acid-activated PM [40], and O2 -• and 1O2-mediated degradation [41]. The influence of pH on SDZ degradation by Vis/PM/CNC-2 system was depicted in Fig. 5b. It demonstrated superior SDZ removal efficiency across a wide pH range from 4 to 9, and the highest degradation efficiency occurred in acidic solutions (99.2 % removal in 30 min at pH = 4). This pH-dependent behavior is attributed to the enhanced oxidative capacity of KMnO4 and intermediate manganese species in acidic media, where the redox potential (E0 = 1.51 V) is significantly higher than in neutral (E0 = 0.58 V) or alkaline (E0 = 0.56 V) environments, facilitating stronger electron transfer and more efficient redox reactions. Notably, even under alkaline conditions (pH = 9), the system achieved 82.7 % SDZ removal within 30 min, demonstrating its robust performance across diverse pH conditions. These results confirm the system’s versatility for organic pollutant degradation under varying environmental pH levels. It may be attributed to the enhanced oxidizing capacity of PM. HCO3 -, SO4 2-, and Cl- exhibited minimal influence on SDZ degradation (Fig. 5c), whereas the addition of HA enhanced the reaction, perhaps it facilitated the reduction of PM and stabilized the generated Mn (III) [9]. Cations such as Ca2+, Zn2+, and Mg2+ had negligible effects on SDZ degradation, but Mn2+ significantly enhanced the degradation (Fig. 5d), likely due to the rapid consumption of PM and generated oxidative Mnint species. From Fig. 5e, the degradation was marginally lower in the tap water and lake water than in the control, suggesting the promising applicability of the Vis/PM/CNC-2 system. To assess the reusability of CNC-2, consecutive experiments were performed after each cycle use of the catalyst. The SDZ removal remained above 90 % even after four cycles (Fig. 5f), suggesting the excellent reusability of CNC-2. These results collectively demonstrate the robustness and effectiveness of the Vis/PM/CNC-2 system for practical applications for pollutant degradation. The XRD and FTIR characterization results of the fresh and used CNC-2 catalysts (Fig. S12) demonstrate that the crystalline phase structure and characteristic functional group peaks of the catalyst remained essentially unchanged. Although a minor shift in the (100) crystal plane was observed, this slight alteration does not compromise the overall structural stability of CNC-2. These findings indicate that CNC-2 maintains excellent structural stability during the photocatalytic process, which is consistent with its outstanding recyclability performance.

In summary, CQD-doped g-C3N4 catalysts (CNC-1 and CNC-2) were prepared and employed as photocatalysts for synergistic PM activation under visible light irradiation. The Vis/PM/CNC-2 system demonstrated superior SDZ degradation efficiencies than those of the Vis/PM, Vis/CNC-2 and Vis/PM/g-C3N4 systems. The CQD-doped CNC-2 catalysts generated more photo-induced e- and h+ under light irradiation. The promoted photogenerated e- reacted with DO to form O2 -• and 1O2, which were the reactive ROS contributors. On the other hand, the photogenerated h+ acted as Lewis acid to elevate its potential for SDZ oxidation. The contribution of Mnint was relatively minor. These findings underscore the effectiveness of CQD-doped g-C₃N₄ catalysts in enhancing PM activation and SDZ degradation under visible light.

Xiaoxia Li: Investigation, Data curation. Aihua Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Shuaiqi Zhao: Investigation, Data curation. Xiuying Liu: Investigation, Data curation. Liansong Gao: Investigation, Data curation. Huangzhao Wei: Investigation, Data curation. Runshi Chen: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Rongrong Hu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51978542) and the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2023YFB3810800).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2025.117162.
